analogy: Think of "Life Processes" through the chapter's own opening images: Science 100 Control and Coordination 6 CHAPTER In the previous chapter, we looked at life processes involved in the maintenance functions in living organisms. There, we had started with a notion we all have, that if we see something moving, it is alive. Some of... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Life Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that science 100 Control and Coordination 6 CHAPTER In the previous chapter, we looked at life processes involved in the maintenance functions in living organisms.

The text explains that there, we had started with a notion we all have, that if we see something moving, it is alive.

The text explains that some of these movements are in fact the result of growth, as in plants.

The text explains that a seed germinates and grows, and we can see that the seedling moves over the course of a few days, it pushes soil aside and comes out.

The text explains that but if its growth were to be stopped, these movements would not happen.

The text explains that some movements, as in many animals and some plants, are not connected with growth.

The text explains that a cat running, children playing on swings, buffaloes chewing cud – these are not movements caused by growth.

The text explains that why do we associate such visible movements with life?

The text explains that a possible answer is that we think of movement as a response to a change in the environment of the organism.

The text explains that the cat may be running because it has seen a mouse.

The text explains that not only that, we also think of movement as an attempt by living organisms to use changes in their environment to their advantage.

The text explains that children try to get pleasure and fun out of swinging.

The text explains that buffaloes chew cud to help break up tough food so as to be able to digest it better.

The text explains that when bright light is focussed on our eyes or when we touch a hot object, we detect the change and respond to it with movement in order to protect ourselves.

The text explains that if we think a bit more about this, it becomes apparent that all this movement, in response to the environment, is carefully controlled.

The text explains that each kind of a change in the environment evokes an appropriate movement in response.

The text explains that all these involuntary actions including blood pressure, salivation and vomiting are controlled by the medulla in the hind-brain.

The text explains that on the other hand, the directional movement of a seedling is caused by growth.

The text explains that more commonly, plants respond to stimuli slowly by growing in a particular direction.

The text explains that because this growth is directional, it appears as if the plant is moving.

The text explains that control and Coordination 107 Environmental triggers such as light, or gravity will change the directions that plant parts grow in.

The text explains that these directional, or tropic, movements can be either towards the stimulus, or away from it.

The text explains that can we think of examples of these kinds of directional growth movements?

The text explains that even in animal bodies, there are carefully controlled directions to growth.

The text explains that our arms and fingers grow in certain directions, not haphazardly.

The text explains that leave it undisturbed in this condition for a few days. n Have the old parts of the shoot and root changed direction? n Are there differences in the direction of the new growth? n What can we conclude from this activity?

The text explains that figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Response of the plant to the direction of light Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Plant showing geotropism

The text explains that adrenaline is secreted directly into the blood and carried to different parts of the body.

The text explains that remember that plants have hormones that control their directional growth.

The text explains that on the face of it, we cannot imagine their role in directional growth.

The text explains that we have never seen an animal growing more in one direction or the other, depending on light or gravity!

The text explains that the gap between two neurons is called a (a) dendrite.

The text explains that what is the function of receptors in our body?

The text explains that think of situations where receptors do not work properly.

The text explains that draw the structure of a neuron and explain its function.

The text explains that which signals will get disrupted in case of a spinal cord injury?

The text explains that how does chemical coordination occur in plants?

The text explains that what is the need for a system of control and coordination in an organism?

The text explains that how are involuntary actions and reflex actions different from each other?

The text explains that compare and contrast nervous and hormonal mechanisms for control and coordination in animals.

The text explains that what is the difference between the manner in which movement takes place in a sensitive plant and the movement in our legs?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 6: Life Processes

schemes.point: In keeping with the general principles of body organisation in multicellular organisms, specialised tissues are used to provide these control and coordination activities. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 1)

schemes.name: Writing
schemes.point: Writing, talking, moving a chair, clapping at the end of a programme are examples of voluntary actions which are based on deciding what to do next. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 3)

schemes.name: They
schemes.point: They are synthesised at places away from where they act and simply diffuse to the area of action. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 8)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6 CHAPTER and 6.1.1 What happens in Reflex Actions? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Life Processes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Life Processes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6 CHAPTER and 6.1.1 What happens in Reflex Actions? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
